threats.title: sponsor() Griefing — Attacker Forces Victim Delegation to Sponsorship Address, Revoking Prize Eligibility
threats.hypothesis_description: The external `sponsor()` function allows anyone to deposit a trivial amount (1 wei) for any receiver and then force that receiver's TWAB delegation to the SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS. This removes the victim's entire delegateBalance, making them ineligible to win prizes until they manually re-delegate.
threats.investigation_rationale: sponsor() has no access control and calls _twabController.sponsor(_receiver) which overrides the receiver's delegation regardless of their current delegation preference. The victim's TWAB delegateBalance drops to zero, affecting their prize eligibility for the observation period.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _twabController.sponsor(_receiver) called unconditionally when receiver is not already sponsoring; no check that msg.sender == _receiver

preconditions: Victim has shares in the vault with active delegation (default self-delegation)
Attacker can approve and deposit 1 wei of underlying asset

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: sponsor() is external with no access control; forces delegation change on _receiver
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any caller can override any user's delegation by depositing dust amount
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that caller == receiver or that receiver consented to sponsorship

analysis_questions: Does the delegation change affect the victim's TWAB for the current period?
Can the victim recover by calling delegate() and how quickly?
Does repeated griefing (re-sponsoring after victim re-delegates) cause permanent TWAB damage?
validation_requirements: Trace delegation flow: sponsor() -> _delegate() -> _transferDelegateBalance()
Verify that the victim's delegateBalance drops to 0 after attack
Test TWAB impact over an observation period

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Attacker approves 1 wei of underlying to Vault. 2. Attacker calls vault.sponsor(1, victim). 3. Victim's delegation is forced to SPONSORSHIP_ADDRESS. 4. Victim's delegateBalance goes to 0. 5. Until victim re-delegates, their TWAB-based prize eligibility is zero.
prerequisites: Vault is collateralized
Attacker has 1 wei of underlying asset

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Victim loses prize eligibility; potential missed prizes worth significant value
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any vault depositor targeted by the attacker
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Victim can re-delegate but must notice the attack; TWAB damage for the affected period is permanent

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for unauthorized sponsor call with arbitrary receiver

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Delegated event emitted but victim must monitor

threats.title: Canary Tier Claim Bypasses Expansion Threshold — Single Claim Triggers Unwarranted Tier Expansion
threats.hypothesis_description: In `_computeNextNumberOfTiers`, the initial value `_nextNumberOfTiers = largestTierClaimed + 2` is computed BEFORE threshold checks. If the canary tier (numberOfTiers-1) is claimed even once, _nextNumberOfTiers = numberOfTiers+1. Since the threshold check only SETS but never REDUCES this value, tier expansion occurs regardless of whether canaryClaimCount and claimCount meet the expansion thresholds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The canary claim sets largestTierClaimed to the canary tier index. The formula `largestTierClaimed + 2` immediately yields numberOfTiers+1. The subsequent if-block requires `_nextNumberOfTiers >= _numTiers` AND both threshold conditions — but even if thresholds fail, _nextNumberOfTiers retains its initial value of numberOfTiers+1.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _nextNumberOfTiers = largestTierClaimed + 2 computed unconditionally; threshold check only increases, never decreases

preconditions: A canary tier prize is claimed in the current draw
lastClosedDrawId > 0 (not the first draw)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Initial _nextNumberOfTiers set from largestTierClaimed + 2; threshold if-block is additive only
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Single canary claim always yields _nextNumberOfTiers = numberOfTiers + 1 regardless of claimExpansionThreshold
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No logic to reduce _nextNumberOfTiers if thresholds aren't met

analysis_questions: Does a single canary claim always trigger expansion even with 0 normal claims?
What is the economic impact of premature tier expansion on prize liquidity?
Can an attacker repeatedly trigger expansion to dilute prizes across many tiers?
validation_requirements: Set up 3-tier pool, claim only one canary prize, verify closeDraw expands to 4 tiers
Verify expansion occurs even when claimCount=0 and canaryClaimCount=1
Measure prize size reduction per expansion step

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Pool has 3 tiers. 2. Attacker claims a single canary prize. 3. largestTierClaimed = 2. 4. closeDraw computes _nextNumberOfTiers = 4 regardless of claim thresholds. 5. Prize liquidity is spread across 4 tiers instead of 3, reducing individual prize sizes. 6. Repeated over multiple draws, tiers expand to maximum, maximizing dilution.
prerequisites: At least one canary tier prize winner exists

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Prize liquidity diluted across excessive tiers; individual prize sizes reduced significantly
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All prize winners receive smaller prizes than intended by the economic model
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Tiers can shrink if canary claims stop, but damage during expansion persists

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for tier expansion with single canary claim and zero normal claims

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: N/A — game theory exploit, not gas attack
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Triggered automatically on closeDraw after any canary claim

threats.title: _estimatedPrizeCount Off-by-One — Incorrect Tier Odds Used for Prize Count Estimation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_estimatedPrizeCount` function maps `numTiers=3` to `ESTIMATED_PRIZES_PER_DRAW_FOR_2_TIERS`, which was computed using 2-tier odds via `estimatedClaimCount(2, 365)`. However, when numberOfTiers=3, the actual tier odds differ from 2-tier odds. This off-by-one means the estimated count is systematically wrong, miscalibrating VRGDA claiming fees and tier expansion thresholds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The generation script computes estimates for numTiers 2-14 using `estimatedClaimCount(numTiers, 365)`. But `_estimatedPrizeCount(3)` returns the 2-tier estimate. Since 2-tier odds differ significantly from 3-tier odds, the estimate is incorrect. This affects Claimer VRGDA fee computation (via `prizePool.estimatedPrizeCount()`) and tier expansion thresholds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: numTiers == 3 returns ESTIMATED_PRIZES_PER_DRAW_FOR_2_TIERS; off-by-one mapping throughout the function

preconditions: Always applies — the off-by-one is a static code defect

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: 2-tier odds are dramatically different from 3-tier odds; estimate for 3 tiers is ~4 but should be ~16
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that constants match actual numberOfTiers

analysis_questions: Calculate the actual expected prize count for each tier configuration vs the constant used
What is the impact on VRGDA fee computation in Claimer?
Does this make tier expansion thresholds unreachable?
validation_requirements: Compare estimatedClaimCount(N, 365) vs ESTIMATED_PRIZES_PER_DRAW_FOR_N_TIERS constants
Verify the mapping numTiers → constant is shifted by 1
Compute actual economic impact on claiming fees

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Not a direct exploit but a systematic miscalibration: VRGDA fees escalate faster than intended (expected 4 claims per draw but ~0.2 actually occur for 3-tier system), making claims expensive for claimers. Prize claiming becomes less economically viable.
prerequisites: System is running with any number of tiers

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Claiming fee miscalibration; prizes may go unclaimed if fees are too high
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All claimers and indirectly all prize winners

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test comparing _estimatedPrizeCount output vs actual expected counts

threats.title: contributePrizeTokens Allows Arbitrary Vault Attribution of Unaccounted Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: contributePrizeTokens has no access control and accepts any `_prizeVault` address. It validates only that `prizeToken.balanceOf(this) - _accountedBalance() >= _amount`. If any unaccounted prize tokens exist in the pool (from direct transfers, rounding remainders, or timing gaps), anyone can call contributePrizeTokens to attribute them to their own vault, increasing their vault portion and winning probability.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contribution is recorded against the provided `_prizeVault` address, not the actual sender of tokens. The delta balance check only ensures tokens exist, not who sent them. In multi-transaction flows or when tokens arrive from non-standard paths, attribution can be hijacked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control; _prizeVault parameter is caller-controlled; validation only checks delta balance existence, not sender

preconditions: Unaccounted prize tokens exist in the PrizePool (delta balance > 0)
Attacker has a vault address they control or can benefit from

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No msg.sender validation or vault registry check; anyone can attribute any unaccounted balance to any vault
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that msg.sender is the vault or authorized

analysis_questions: How often do unaccounted tokens accumulate in the PrizePool?
Can an attacker reliably find delta balance > 0?
Does this affect vault portion calculations enough to change winner outcomes?
validation_requirements: Send prize tokens directly to PrizePool, then call contributePrizeTokens from different address
Verify vault accumulator credited for the attacker's vault
Test winner determination with inflated vault portion

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Prize tokens arrive at PrizePool via direct transfer (e.g., someone mistakenly sends tokens). 2. Attacker observes delta balance > 0. 3. Attacker calls contributePrizeTokens(attackerVault, deltaBalance). 4. Attacker's vault gets credit, inflating their vault portion. 5. Attacker's users have higher winning probability.
prerequisites: Unaccounted tokens in PrizePool

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Stolen contribution credit inflates attacker's vault portion; legitimate vaults lose proportional prize share
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All other vault depositors through diluted winning probabilities
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Contribution credit is permanent in accumulator

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for unauthorized vault attribution

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: ContributePrizeTokens event shows vault and amount

threats.title: TwabController mint/burn/transfer are permissionless per-vault namespace: any caller can forge balances for their own 'vault' address and potentially deceive integrations that don't authenticate vault contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: TwabController uses msg.sender as the vault key for all balance mutations, but does not restrict who may call mint/burn/transfer. While intended (each vault contract self-manages its namespace), any EOA/contract can create a fake vault namespace with arbitrary TWAB histories. If any downstream system (or PrizePool deployment) mistakenly treats TwabController balances as authoritative without validating that the vault is a legitimate Vault instance for a PrizePool, an attacker could fabricate balances/supplies to pass eligibility checks or manipulate odds calculations for that fake vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a common integration footgun: the controller is globally shared and does not authenticate vaults. The PrizePool currently accepts arbitrary msg.sender as the vault in claimPrize, and calls TwabController for that vault. If deployments do not enforce that only approved vaults can claim/contribute, a fake vault could interact with PrizePool flows in unexpected ways (e.g., winning checks based on attacker-controlled TWAB).
deployment_assumptions: PrizePool (or off-chain systems) do not enforce an allowlist/registry of valid vaults for claims and/or contributions
At least one real integration assumes TwabController state implies legitimacy of a vault address
threats.config_evidence: No vault registry/allowlist is present in PrizePool.sol; claimPrize uses msg.sender as vault without validation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mint/burn/transfer are external and call _transferBalance(msg.sender, ...) using msg.sender as vault key; no access control

preconditions: A downstream consumer (PrizePool or other) accepts arbitrary vault addresses (msg.sender) without registry validation
Attacker can call TwabController.mint/burn/transfer from an arbitrary address (EOA/contract) to shape balances in their own namespace

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: permissionless state mutation keyed by msg.sender namespace
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: fake vaults can manufacture TWAB state; if PrizePool doesn't authenticate vaults, winner eligibility can be computed on attacker-controlled balances/supply
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No vault allowlist in PrizePool; no access control in TwabController

analysis_questions: Does PrizePool intend to accept claims only from Vault.sol instances? If yes, where is that enforced?
Are there deployment-time registries/routers not shown that gate claimPrize/contributePrizeTokens to real vaults?
Can an attacker create a contract that calls PrizePool.claimPrize as msg.sender=vault and also controls TwabController state for that vault to satisfy isWinner?
validation_requirements: End-to-end exploit test: deploy FakeVault, mint TWAB balances via TwabController, attempt PrizePool.claimPrize from FakeVault
Check whether PrizePool relies on vaultAccumulator contributions for vaultPortion (can be manipulated by attacker contributions)
Confirm any off-chain allowlisting assumptions in deployment scripts (none provided)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys FakeVault that (a) calls TwabController.mint to give attacker huge balance and total supply in the FakeVault namespace, (b) contributes prize tokens to PrizePool under FakeVault to create non-zero vaultPortion, then (c) calls PrizePool.claimPrize as FakeVault. If PrizePool doesn't authenticate vaults, the winner check is run against attacker-controlled TWABs and may pass, letting attacker siphon prize liquidity intended for real vault ecosystem.
prerequisites: Ability to contribute prize tokens (capital requirement) to establish FakeVault portion
No vault authentication in PrizePool deployment

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential prize token drain from PrizePool tiers/reserve proportional to contributed liquidity and odds manipulation within fake vault namespace
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All PrizePool participants if fake vault claims can be executed

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing tests ensuring only legitimate vaults can call claimPrize / that PrizePool validates vault authenticity

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Requires prize token contributions; quantify minimal contribution needed to produce winningZone > 0
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-transaction setup; then single tx claim attempt
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain fake vault interactions should be observable; require monitoring to detect unauthorized vault addresses claiming

threats.title: PrizePool.claimPrize allows arbitrary msg.sender as vault: absence of vault authentication may enable claims from non-Vault contracts (spoofed vault) and breaks trust boundary assumptions
threats.hypothesis_description: PrizePool.claimPrize treats msg.sender as the vault and uses it across claimedPrizes bookkeeping and TWAB queries. There is no explicit validation that msg.sender is an approved Vault instance, nor that it corresponds to the PrizePool's intended vault set. If a non-vault contract can satisfy the isWinner predicate (by controlling TwabController state for its own address and contributing prize tokens attributed to itself), it could claim prizes despite not being a legitimate vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: Phase A highlighted TwabController namespaces and permissionless contributePrizeTokens attribution. Combining those with claimPrize's use of msg.sender as vault is a practical composability risk unless a registry exists elsewhere. This threatens fairness and may violate deployment assumptions about 'permissionless vaults' being created via VaultFactory with correct wiring.
deployment_assumptions: No external registry/allowlist is enforced by DrawManager/Liquidator layer to restrict which vaults can meaningfully participate
Attackers can create a contract that calls claimPrize directly
threats.config_evidence: PrizePool.sol contains no mapping/setter enforcing an allowed vault set; deployment scripts for gating not provided.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimPrize uses msg.sender as vault; no validation that msg.sender is a known Vault; TWAB queries use _vault parameter directly

preconditions: Attacker can arrange non-zero vaultPortion for their chosen vault address (msg.sender) via contributePrizeTokens attribution
Attacker can influence TWAB state for that vault address (e.g., via TwabController namespace control from that address)
No off-chain or draw-manager enforced vault allowlist exists

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: any contract can be a 'vault' for claim bookkeeping and TWAB, enabling spoofed vault participation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No allowlist, no interface check, no VaultFactory registry usage

analysis_questions: Is there an expected external registry (out of scope) that constrains vaults? If so, what happens if it's bypassed?
Can a contract both modify TwabController balances for itself and successfully call claimPrize to pass isWinner?
Do PrizePool tiers assume a bounded set of vaults for contribution smoothing?
validation_requirements: Build FakeVault PoC that calls claimPrize and can satisfy isWinner
Check if claimedPrizes mapping isolation (by vault) prevents interference but still allows unauthorized extraction
Confirm whether contributePrizeTokens + claimPrize combination can create profitable expected value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys contract V, engineers TWAB state and contributions for V, then calls claimPrize as V for attacker address. If isWinner passes, PrizePool pays out prize tokens to attacker even though V is not a real vault with depositor principal.
prerequisites: Some prize liquidity exists in tiers
Attacker provides sufficient contributions to create non-zero winning zone

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Material prize liquidity theft or unfair extraction by unauthorized vaults
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All legitimate vault depositors (diluted prize chances / depleted liquidity)

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests indicated for restricting claimPrize caller identity

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Quantify minimum contribution required vs expected winnings
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor claimPrize events for unknown vault addresses

threats.title: Vault.withdraw/redeem may be reentrancy-reachable via ERC777 hooks during asset transfer, enabling nested withdrawals before share accounting settles in external yieldVault
threats.hypothesis_description: Vault._withdraw burns shares first, then calls yieldVault.withdraw, then transfers underlying asset to receiver. If the underlying asset is ERC777 (or ERC677-like) the transfer to receiver can trigger callbacks on receiver that re-enter Vault to call withdraw/redeem again. While shares are already burned for the first call, reentrancy could still exploit any intermediate inconsistencies (e.g., yieldVault state not yet updated as expected, or reliance on Vault's transient asset balance) or create DoS/edge-accounting issues.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract comments discuss ERC777 ordering but do not add a reentrancy guard. Need to confirm there is no other mutable shared state (e.g., exchange rate updates, approvals, yield fee supply) that can be exploited in a nested call chain or to manipulate recorded exchange rate.
deployment_assumptions: Underlying asset implements ERC777 hooks or otherwise re-enters on transfer
Receiver is a contract that can perform reentrant calls
threats.config_evidence: Vault supports arbitrary IERC20 asset passed at construction; no explicit ERC20-only restriction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _withdraw burns shares, then external call yieldVault.withdraw, then external call asset.transfer to receiver; no nonReentrant

preconditions: Asset token can trigger reentrancy on transfer (ERC777 tokensReceived on receiver)
Receiver is attacker-controlled contract
No external invariant in yieldVault that blocks nested withdrawals (e.g., shares/accounting mismatch)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external calls in sequence without reentrancy guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: nested withdraw/redeem could interact with exchangeRate updates or transient balances
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier; no pull-pattern for transfers

analysis_questions: Can reentrancy reach withdraw/redeem in a way that bypasses allowance/share checks?
Does _updateExchangeRate after burn create exploitable rate changes mid-withdraw chain?
Does yieldVault.withdraw reentrancy into Vault lead to inconsistencies with _totalAssets/_currentExchangeRate?
validation_requirements: Write ERC777 mock that re-enters withdraw/redeem on tokensReceived
Assert conservation: total assets + shares supply after nested calls
Fuzz test nested sequences withdraw->redeem->withdraw

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker withdraws to a contract receiver; during asset transfer callback, receiver re-enters Vault to attempt additional withdrawals/redeems based on stale perceived exchange rate or transient balances, aiming to extract extra assets or force inconsistent exchange rate recording.
prerequisites: ERC777-like asset or hook-enabled wrapper token
Attacker has shares to withdraw and a malicious receiver contract

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential partial drain or accounting corruption; at minimum, withdrawal path DoS or exchange rate manipulation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium; add reentrancy guard and harden ordering

threats.test_coverage_gap: Likely missing ERC777 reentrancy tests on withdraw/redeem

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Look for repeated Withdraw events in one tx / anomalous exchangeRate changes

threats.title: TieredLiquidityDistributor.getTierRemainingLiquidity / getTierPrizeSize missing tier bounds checks may allow out-of-range reads that mislead integrations and automated claimers
threats.hypothesis_description: Several external view functions accept a _tier without validating _tier < numberOfTiers (e.g., getTierRemainingLiquidity, getTierPrizeSize). Out-of-range tiers will read default-initialized Tier structs from the mapping and compute values that may appear valid (often 0), potentially misleading off-chain automation (claimers, UIs) into skipping real tiers or miscomputing max fee tiers.
threats.investigation_rationale: While view-only, these functions influence Claimer fee cap logic and operator decisions. If a state-changing path relies on these views (e.g., Claimer.computeMaxFee uses prizePool.getTierPrizeSize), mismatches could result in fee mispricing and failed claims (economic DoS).

proof_of_signal.pattern: external views call _getTier/_getTierRemainingLiquidity without explicit _tier < numberOfTiers check; mapping read returns default struct

preconditions: Off-chain systems or contracts call these getters with untrusted or computed tier indices
Caller uses returned values to make decisions (e.g., fee caps, claim routing)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: missing bounds validation on user-supplied index
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: default Tier struct can mask invalid tier as zero-liquidity / zero prize size
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No revert on invalid tier for these getters (unlike PrizePool._checkValidTier used elsewhere)

analysis_questions: Are these getters used by on-chain logic (e.g., Claimer) with tier values that could be invalid?
Could a malicious caller cause automated claim bots to misbehave by feeding invalid tiers through public endpoints?
Should these functions mirror PrizePool invalid-tier behavior (revert) for safety?
validation_requirements: Trace all internal/external call sites (on-chain) that rely on these getters
Simulate Claimer.computeMaxFee and claimPrizes for boundary tiers (0, numTiers-1, numTiers, 255)
Confirm whether any state-changing path is blocked/mispriced due to invalid tier getter behavior

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker spams automation endpoints with invalid tiers, causing bots to compute fee caps as 0 and skip claiming or submit reverting transactions repeatedly, delaying real claims.
prerequisites: Presence of third-party automated claimers relying on these views
No robust input validation in bot layer

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect: delayed claims, increased operational costs, potentially lower protocol liveness
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: High; add bounds checks or document strict usage

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for invalid tier inputs on TieredLiquidityDistributor getters

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor for invalid-tier RPC calls / bot failures

threats.title: DrawAccumulatorLib.getDisbursedBetween hard limit on _endDrawId may revert and block critical state-changing paths (PrizePool.reserveForOpenDraw / contribution accounting) under certain draw ID patterns
threats.hypothesis_description: getDisbursedBetween intentionally reverts if _endDrawId < newestObservedDrawId - 1. PrizePool uses getDisbursedBetween in state-changing contexts (closeDraw via _contributionsForDraw, contributePrizeTokens via add sequences, and reserveForOpenDraw view but used operationally). If a caller supplies ranges that become invalid due to ring buffer evolution, key protocol actions may revert unexpectedly, potentially blocking draw closures if internal computed ranges ever violate the constraint.
threats.investigation_rationale: Although intended as a gas optimization, this restriction can become a liveness hazard if any internal computation requests disbursement over an older endDrawId. Need to verify all internal call sites always satisfy the constraint, especially around first draws, missed draws, and smoothing effects.

proof_of_signal.pattern: revert InvalidDisbursedEndDrawId if _endDrawId < drawIds.second - 1; used by PrizePool internal computations

preconditions: Internal or external call path requests getDisbursedBetween with an endDrawId more than 1 behind newest observation
That call is part of a state-changing operation (e.g., closeDraw) or blocks critical operation

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: intentional revert constraint on historical queries
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: unexpected revert could brick closeDraw or contribution-related operations if computed ranges slip behind
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No defensive clamping in PrizePool call sites shown

analysis_questions: Do PrizePool internal calls ever pass endDrawId that can be older than newest-1 (e.g., during bootstrapping or missed draws)?
Can an attacker influence ring buffer newest draw id such that legitimate operations become invalid?
Are there safe wrappers or try/catch patterns around these calls?
validation_requirements: Trace all PrizePool call sites to getDisbursedBetween and prove endDrawId invariants
Simulate ring buffer near-capacity and missed draws, ensure _contributionsForDraw always queries valid windows
Fuzz start/end draw IDs used in _computeVaultTierDetails and _getVaultPortion usage

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If internal computations accidentally query an out-of-date range after long inactivity or ring buffer wrap, closeDraw reverts, halting protocol. Alternatively, an attacker could attempt to manipulate contribution observation draw IDs (via permitted contributePrizeTokens patterns) to push newest draw and invalidate older endDrawId queries.
prerequisites: Sufficient number of observations to approach wrap-around / high drawId values
Operational patterns that query historical ranges

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol liveness loss (draw closures blocked) leading to prize stagnation and user harm
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium; requires code adjustment or operational workaround

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing adversarial tests around InvalidDisbursedEndDrawId and internal PrizePool call ranges

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor for systematic closeDraw reverts due to InvalidDisbursedEndDrawId

threats.title: mintYieldFee Missing Access Control Allows Anyone to Steal Accrued Yield Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: The Vault.mintYieldFee function is external with NO access control and ignores the _yieldFeeRecipient state variable. Any caller can mint accrued yield fee shares (_yieldFeeTotalSupply) to any arbitrary recipient address. This enables direct theft of all accumulated yield fees.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear access control gap: the function should be restricted to the _yieldFeeRecipient or the owner, but neither is checked. The _yieldFeeRecipient address is completely ignored in the minting logic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: external function with no access modifier; _yieldFeeRecipient state var is never referenced in mintYieldFee; any address can be _recipient

preconditions: _yieldFeeTotalSupply > 0 (yield fees have been accumulated via liquidation)
_yieldFeePercentage > 0 (yield fee is active)
Vault is collateralized (_currentExchangeRate >= _assetUnit)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: mintYieldFee takes arbitrary _recipient, checks only _shares <= _yieldFeeTotalSupply and collateralization
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls mintYieldFee(_yieldFeeTotalSupply, attackerAddress) to mint all accrued fee shares to themselves
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals entire _yieldFeeTotalSupply worth of vault shares, redeemable for underlying assets

economic_questions: What is the expected _yieldFeeTotalSupply accumulation rate for a given vault?
Can the attacker front-run the legitimate yield fee recipient's mint call?
validation_requirements: Confirm no hidden access control inherited from parent contracts
Calculate expected yield fee accumulation per draw period

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All accrued yield fees (depends on vault TVL and fee percentage)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after any liquidation that increases _yieldFeeTotalSupply
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Reduces vault yield for all depositors; yield fee recipient receives nothing

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Yield fee recipient loses all accrued fees; vault depositors indirectly affected by dilution
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Entire yield fee allocation stolen
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All accrued _yieldFeeTotalSupply across all vaults with active yield fees

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no access control on mintYieldFee; confirm _yieldFeeRecipient is never checked

threats.title: Reverting Winner Hooks Cause Batch Claim DoS and Gas Griefing of Claimers
threats.hypothesis_description: Winners can set arbitrary hook implementations via setHooks. A malicious hook that reverts in beforeClaimPrize or afterClaimPrize causes the entire batch claim transaction (processing multiple winners) in Vault.claimPrizes to revert. This griefs claimers who waste gas and prevents other legitimate winners in the same batch from receiving their prizes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Vault._claimPrize function makes external calls to user-controlled hook contracts without try/catch protection. A single reverting hook poisons an entire batch of claims.

proof_of_signal.pattern: hooks.implementation.beforeClaimPrize and afterClaimPrize called without try/catch; revert propagates to batch claimPrizes caller

preconditions: Winner sets a hook with useBeforeClaimPrize=true or useAfterClaimPrize=true
Hook implementation reverts on call
Claimer includes the malicious winner in a batch claim

threats.title: Vault _deposit Skips Token Transfer When Vault Holds Idle Assets, Enabling Free Share Minting
threats.hypothesis_description: In Vault._deposit, when the vault's underlying asset balance (_vaultAssets) is >= the deposit amount (_assets), NO tokens are transferred from the caller. The vault uses its existing idle balance to deposit into the yield vault, and mints shares to the receiver for free. This allows theft of any underlying assets sitting in the vault contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _deposit function has an explicit code path where _assets <= _vaultAssets results in no safeTransferFrom call, yet full share minting occurs. This is a clear code defect where the depositor receives shares without payment.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_assets > _vaultAssets) { ... transfer ... } — else branch does no transfer but still calls _yieldVault.deposit and _mint

threats.hypothesis_description: In Vault.liquidate, the condition `_amountOut >= _vaultAssets` compares _amountOut (denominated in vault shares) with _vaultAssets (denominated in underlying assets). This causes the yield vault deposit sweep to not trigger, leaving underlying assets stranded in the vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear unit mismatch bug. It enables assets to accumulate in the vault, directly feeding the _deposit free share vulnerability (ECON-H005).

threats.hypothesis_description: The `_currentExchangeRate` function permanently caps the exchange rate at `_lastRecordedExchangeRate`. If the yield vault suffers a loss, the exchange rate drops. threats.title: UD34x4 Truncation Inflates Reserve, Allowing DrawManager to Steal Active Tier Liquidity
threats.hypothesis_description: When liquidity is consumed, the conversion to `UD34x4` truncates the value, causing `prizeTokenPerShare` to increase by less than it should. When tiers are reclaimed, this rounding error causes the tier to report more remaining liquidity than it actually has, which is then added to the reserve. The DrawManager can withdraw this inflated reserve, stealing tokens that back active tiers.
threats.investigation_rationale: Integer division by `1e14` in `fromUD60x18toUD34x4` rounds down. Over thousands of claims, this phantom liquidity accumulates in the reserve. Withdrawing the reserve breaks the solvency invariant, causing future legitimate prize claims to revert.

threats.title: TwabController.delegate(vault, address(0)) Permanently Locks User Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: Calling TwabController.delegate(vault, address(0)) sets the user's delegateBalance to 0 while leaving their balance intact. The _delegateOf function then returns the user's own address (interpreting address(0) as self-delegation), causing subsequent burn/transfer operations to attempt decreasing delegateBalance by the transfer amount. Since delegateBalance is 0, these operations revert, permanently locking the user's funds. Re-delegation is also blocked because _transferDelegateBalance tries to decrease the already-zero delegateBalance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The delegate function lacks a check preventing address(0) as a target. When _delegateOf converts address(0) to user address, it creates an inconsistency: the system treats the user as self-delegating (expects delegateBalance == balance), but delegateBalance was already zeroed by the delegation transfer. This permanently breaks all balance-modifying operations for the user.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _delegateOf returns user when delegates is address(0), but delegate(address(0)) zeroes delegateBalance via _transferDelegateBalance. Subsequent self-delegate operations assume delegateBalance tracks balance but it's permanently 0.

preconditions: User must have a non-zero balance in a vault tracked by TwabController
User calls TwabController.delegate(vaultAddress, address(0))

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit for attacker; self-inflicted permanent fund lock. Impact is total loss of locked tokens for the affected user.

threats.title: TWAB Timestamp Underflow Blocks Prize Claims for Early Draws
threats.hypothesis_description: In _getVaultUserBalanceAndTotalSupplyTwab, the start timestamp is computed as uint32(_endTimestamp - _drawDuration * drawPeriodSeconds). For tiers with high draw durations (e.g., tier 0 with ~365 draws, tier 1 with ~19 draws), this subtraction underflows during early protocol operation because insufficient draws have occurred. Solidity 0.8's checked arithmetic causes a revert, making prizes for these tiers unclaimable for an extended initial period.
threats.investigation_rationale: The draw duration for tier 0 is ~365 draws (one year of daily draws). The start timestamp subtraction requires at least 365 draws to have occurred before tier 0 prizes can be claimed. For tier 1 with 3 tiers, draw duration is ~19, blocking claims for ~19 days. This locks allocated prize liquidity that cannot be claimed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint32 _startTimestamp = uint32(_endTimestamp - _drawDuration * drawPeriodSeconds) underflows when _drawDuration * drawPeriodSeconds > _endTimestamp for early draws

preconditions: Protocol is within its first N draws (N varies by tier: ~365 for grand prize, ~19 for tier 1)

threats.title: DrawAccumulatorLib Rounding Allows Vault Contribution Fraction > 1.0
threats.hypothesis_description: The _getVaultPortion function computes vaultContributed / totalContributed using getDisbursedBetween on separate accumulators. Both accumulators receive the same inputs via contributePrizeTokens, but getDisbursedBetween performs independent exponential math (pow, integrate, integrateInf) using SD59x18 operations on potentially different observation states. Rounding differences can cause vaultContributed > totalContributed, yielding a vault portion exceeding 1.0. This inflates the winning zone in isWinner, allowing more prizes to be won than the vault's liquidity allocation supports.
threats.investigation_rationale: The vault and total accumulators are updated independently. While they receive the same contribution amounts, the exponential smoothing computations in getDisbursedBetween involve pow() and integrate() operations that can produce different rounding based on the accumulator's observation history. If a single vault is the dominant contributor, any rounding where vaultDisbursed > totalDisbursed creates vaultPortion > 1.0, directly inflating winning zones.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Independent SD59x18 pow/integrate computations on vault vs total accumulators with different observation states can produce different rounding, allowing vaultContributed > totalContributed

preconditions: A vault is a dominant contributor (large fraction of total contributions)
Smoothing parameter is non-zero (enabling exponential math divergence)
Accumulator observation histories differ between vault and total (e.g., different contribution timing patterns)

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extra prize claims drain liquidity beyond the vault's fair share, stealing from other vaults' allocations or the reserve

threats.title: Claimer Fee Computation Uses Stale claimCount Leading to Fee Underpayment
threats.hypothesis_description: In Claimer.claimPrizes, feePerClaim is computed using prizePool.claimCount() read at function entry. The VRGDA fee depends on the number of already-claimed prizes (_claimedCount). If another transaction claims prizes between the Claimer's read and write, the fee computation is based on stale data. With a lower claimCount, the VRGDA computes a lower fee, meaning the claimer pays less than the intended economic rate. Conversely, the claimer could overpay if competing claims reduced remaining unclaimed prizes.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Claimer reads claimCount once, computes the average fee across all claims in the batch, then executes the claims. On L2s without public mempools, two independent claimers could submit transactions in the same block. The second transaction's fee computation doesn't account for the first transaction's claims. The VRGDA pricing depends on elapsed time and sold count, so stale sold count produces incorrect prices.

proof_of_signal.pattern: prizePool.claimCount() read once at line ~77, then used for fee computation. Actual claimCount may change between read and vault.claimPrizes execution

preconditions: Multiple claimers submit transactions in the same block or close succession
First claimer's transaction changes claimCount before second claimer's execution

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Two claimers in same block: second claimer's fee is computed as if fewer claims occurred, potentially under-pricing or over-pricing the fee
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Minimal direct profit but could lead to systematic fee underpayment by coordinated claiming

threats.title: Vault Redeem Can Return Zero Assets for Non-Zero Shares Due to Rounding
threats.hypothesis_description: In Vault.redeem, assets are computed via _convertToAssets(_shares, Math.Rounding.Down). When the exchange rate is very low (e.g., after severe undercollateralization) or share amounts are small, the rounding-down conversion can produce _assets = 0 for non-zero _shares. The function then calls _withdraw with _assets = 0, burning the user's shares but transferring 0 underlying assets. There is no check for _assets > 0 or minimum withdrawal amount, resulting in silent value destruction.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _convertToAssets function: _shares.mulDiv(_exchangeRate, _assetUnit, Rounding.Down). When _shares * _exchangeRate < _assetUnit, the result rounds to 0. The user burns shares (permanent loss of TWAB position and vault token) but receives nothing. This could affect dust amounts normally, but during undercollateralization the threshold increases significantly.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 _assets = _convertToAssets(_shares, Math.Rounding.Down); followed by _withdraw without checking _assets > 0

preconditions: User holds a small number of vault shares

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Burned shares without asset redemption benefit remaining shareholders marginally

threats.title: Vault _withdraw Allows Exit During Undercollateralization Creating Unfair Loss Socialization
threats.hypothesis_description: Vault.withdraw and redeem have no collateralization check, allowing exits during undercollateralization at the deflated exchange rate. While maxDeposit returns 0 (blocking deposits), withdrawals proceed freely. Early withdrawers absorb a smaller fraction of the loss than late withdrawers since the exchange rate degrades further as the vault's asset-to-share ratio worsens with each withdrawal. This creates a bank run incentive where informed users exit first, socializing losses disproportionately to slower users.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetry between deposit (blocked when undercollateralized) and withdraw (always allowed) creates a one-directional flow during crises. Each withdrawal reduces _withdrawableAssets and _totalSupply, but the exchange rate formula (_withdrawableAssets * _assetUnit / _totalSupply) doesn't necessarily degrade linearly. If yield vault losses are proportional to holdings, withdrawals don't change the rate. But if losses are fixed-amount (e.g., exploit), early withdrawers get a better rate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw and redeem proceed without _requireVaultCollateralized check, unlike deposit/mint/liquidate which block during undercollateralization

preconditions: Yield vault suffers a loss causing vault undercollateralization
Multiple users compete to withdraw
Loss is not perfectly proportional to all holders (e.g., fixed-amount loss)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Exchange rate = _withdrawableAssets * _assetUnit / _totalSupply. After fixed loss L: rate = (Total - L) / Supply. After withdrawal of S shares: rate = (Total - L - withdrawn) / (Supply - S). If L/Total < S/Supply, rate improves for remaining users; otherwise degrades.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Informed users (bots, whales) withdraw immediately upon detecting yield vault loss. Retail users with slower reaction lose a disproportionate share.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early withdrawer extracts more assets per share than late withdrawer. With 10% loss and 50% early exit, late users bear ~20% loss instead of 10%.

threats.title: PrizePool._openDrawEndsAt Skips Draws Silently, Losing Contributions
threats.hypothesis_description: The _openDrawEndsAt function handles missed draws by calculating how many draw periods have been skipped and offsetting the end time. When closeDraw is finally called, lastClosedDrawId increments by exactly 1 (via _computeNewDistributions). But contributions made during the skipped draws were accumulated for specific draw IDs. The single closeDraw call only processes contributions for lastClosedDrawId + 1, meaning contributions earmarked for skipped draws may not be properly distributed. The skipped draw IDs never get closed, and their contributions may become inaccessible or incorrectly attributed.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the draw manager fails to close draws for multiple periods, _openDrawEndsAt jumps ahead. But closeDraw only increments the draw ID by 1. The draw accumulator has contributions indexed by draw ID. If draw IDs 5, 6, 7 are skipped and draw 8 is closed as the 'next' draw, contributions for draws 5-7 may not be properly disbursed. The getDisbursedBetween function is used to calculate contributions for the closing draw, which would only cover draw 8, potentially excluding smoothed contributions from skipped draws.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _openDrawEndsAt adds drawPeriodSeconds * numMissedDraws to skip ahead, but closeDraw increments lastClosedDrawId by exactly 1. Draw IDs in between are never closed.

preconditions: Draw manager fails to close draws for at least 2 draw periods
Contributions were made during the skipped draw periods

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: closeDraw processes: closedDrawId = lastClosedDrawId + 1. After missing N draws, closedDrawId is still lastClosedDrawId + 1, not lastClosedDrawId + N. _contributionsForDraw(lastClosedDrawId + 1) gets contributions smoothed into that single draw, but the exponential smoothing may not correctly account for the time gap.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Draw manager (or attacker who front-ran setDrawManager) intentionally delays draw closure to disrupt contribution accounting
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Skipped draws cause contribution misaccounting. Vaults that contributed during skipped periods lose their fair share of prize distribution.

Are contributions for skipped draw IDs lost or redistributed?

Check if contribution amounts are preserved across draw skips

threats.title: yieldFeePercentage = FEE_PRECISION (100%) Causes Division by Zero in liquidate()
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_setYieldFeePercentage` function allows setting the fee to exactly `FEE_PRECISION` (1e9) since the check uses `>` instead of `>=`. In `liquidate()`, the yield fee calculation `(_amountOut * FEE_PRECISION) / (FEE_PRECISION - _yieldFeePercentage)` produces division by zero when `_yieldFeePercentage == FEE_PRECISION`, permanently bricking the liquidation mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The boundary check `if (yieldFeePercentage_ > FEE_PRECISION)` allows the value FEE_PRECISION to pass. When set, the denominator in liquidate's yield fee calculation becomes zero. Since liquidation is the only mechanism to convert yield to prizes, this permanently halts prize generation.

threats.title: TieredLiquidityDistributor prize size computation may produce zero/incorrect prize sizes for unsupported tier/numTiers combinations, breaking claims and causing operational dead zones
threats.hypothesis_description: Tier odds and canary prize count fractionals are hardcoded only for numTiers in [3..15]. If any pathway (misconfiguration or future extension) sets numberOfTiers outside these bounds or calls getTierOdds/estimatedPrizeCount with out-of-range values, odds return sd(0) and estimated prize count returns 0, which can drive winner calculations toward always-false and break fee computations/claim incentives. Although bounds are enforced in constructor/_nextDraw, operational tooling and external callers may rely on view outputs for off-chain automation and could mis-handle zeros, causing failed closeDraw/claimer pricing decisions.
